title: Cat Weight Loss Despite Increased Appetite: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat eating more but losing weight? Learn about common causes like hyperthyroidism and diabetes, and understand when to contact your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden weight loss despite an increased appetite is a medium-priority medical concern that typically requires a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours. This combination of symptoms often indicates metabolic conditions like hyperthyroidism or diabetes that require professional diagnostic testing.

What does it mean if my cat is losing weight even though they are eating more than usual?

When a cat experiences weight loss despite eating more than usual, it often indicates a metabolic or malabsorptive disorder. In these cases, the body is either burning through calories at an unsustainable rate or is unable to properly process and absorb the nutrients from the food provided. Common underlying conditions include hyperthyroidism (an overactive thyroid gland), diabetes mellitus, or gastrointestinal issues such as inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) or intestinal parasites.

Is sudden weight loss with an increased appetite considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for sudden weight loss with an increased appetite is classified as Medium.
  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours rather than waiting for a routine check-up weeks away.
  • Shift to high urgency and seek immediate care if your cat stops eating entirely, begins vomiting frequently, or becomes extremely lethargic.

How can taking a photo of my cat help with the triage process?

  • Providing a photo helps the veterinarian or triage professional accurately assess the severity of the weight loss and muscle wasting.
  • Take a "bird's eye view" photo by looking down at your cat's back while they are standing to highlight the tuck of the waist.
  • Visual data helps the clinical team prioritize your cat's case and prepare the necessary diagnostic tests before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Weight loss despite increased appetite (polyphagia) in cats can be indicative of several underlying conditions. Common causes include hyperthyroidism, where other signs such as hyperexcitability, polydipsia, polyuria, and possible thyroid gland enlargement may be present; although some cats may exhibit apathetic signs such as lethargy. Gastrointestinal diseases causing malabsorption should also be considered, characterized by chronic diarrhea, altered appetite (anorexia or polyphagia), and potentially coprophagia or pica, and thickening of bowel loops may be palpable.

Chapter: Endocrinology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 559)

faqs.question: Can intestinal parasites cause weight loss in adult cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, heavy loads of parasites like roundworms or tapeworms can consume enough nutrients to cause weight loss even if the cat is eating well, though this is more common in outdoor cats or kittens.

faqs.question: Is hyperthyroidism common in older cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, hyperthyroidism is one of the most common glandular disorders in senior cats and frequently presents as weight loss paired with a frantic or 'ravenous' appetite.

faqs.question: What diagnostic tests will my vet likely recommend?
faqs.answer: Your vet will likely recommend a 'Senior Wellness Screen,' which includes a chemistry panel, a complete blood count (CBC), a thyroid level check (T4), and a urinalysis to rule out diabetes and kidney disease.
